mnemonics.title: Cloud Service Models
mnemonics.content: IaaS: Infrastructure - You manage OS, apps. PaaS: Platform - You manage app. SaaS: Software - Provider manages everything. Think: 'I Paint Swords' (Infrastructure → Platform → Software) for IaaS → PaaS → SaaS progression from most user control to least.

mnemonics.title: NIST Cloud Characteristics
mnemonics.content: On-Demand, Self-Service, Resource Pooling, Rapid Elasticity, Measured Service. Mnemonic: 'ORREM' (Oh Really? Real Elastic Magic!) or remember as 'S.P.O.O.N' - Self-service, Pooling, On-demand, Elasticity, Measured.

mnemonics.title: Deployment Models
mnemonics.content: Public (Everyone shares), Private (One org), Hybrid (Mix), Community (Shared interests). Mnemonic: 'P P H C' - Public/Private/Hybrid/Community - remember as 'People Prefer Hybrid Coffee'.

acronyms.usage: Provides virtualized computing resources like servers, storage, and networking. Examples: AWS EC2, Google Compute Engine.
Provides development platforms with tools for building, testing, and deploying applications. Examples: Google App Engine, Heroku.
Delivers software applications over the internet on a subscription basis. Examples: Gmail, Salesforce, Microsoft 365.

acronyms.expansion: National Institute of Standards and Technology
acronyms.usage: U.S. agency that defined the standard cloud computing definition and essential characteristics.
Contract defining guaranteed performance metrics (uptime, response time) between provider and customer.
Allows applications to interact with cloud services programmatically for automation.
Software emulation of a physical computer running an operating system, created by hypervisors.
Distributed network of servers that delivers content to users based on their geographic location.

visualTips.visualization: Draw an inverted pyramid: Bottom (largest) = IaaS with most user control, Middle = PaaS, Top (smallest) = SaaS with least user control but most managed services.
Picture a large office building (data center) with many companies (tenants) sharing the same building but having locked, private offices (isolated data).
Think of a shared restaurant kitchen where ingredients (computing resources) from multiple sources are combined and allocated to different dishes (user requests) as needed.
Imagine an accordion that expands when more music notes (workload) are played and compresses when quiet - cloud resources work the same way.

analogies.analogy: Like moving from owning a personal water pump (traditional IT - buy, maintain, fixed capacity) to city water supply (cloud - pay for what you use, scalable, no maintenance).
IaaS = renting a bare apartment (furnish it yourself); PaaS = renting a furnished apartment (move in with your clothes); SaaS = staying at a hotel (everything provided).
Like a magic mirror that splits into many mirrors, each showing a different room - one physical server becomes many virtual servers.
Like a traffic police officer directing cars at an intersection, ensuring no lane gets too congested - distributing work evenly.
